description: Count distinct active users per day or per month using timestamp truncation and COUNT DISTINCT. The foundation of all product health metrics at Meta.
schema: user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_type VARCHAR, event_ts TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Write a query that returns the Monthly Active Users (MAU) for each calendar month in 2024, along with the peak Daily Active Users (DAU) observed within that month.

description: Measure conversion rates between sequential steps in a user journey (e.g., impression → click → signup → purchase). Requires counting users who reached each step and dividing by the prior step's count.
schema: user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_type VARCHAR, event_ts TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Given events 'page_view', 'add_to_cart', 'checkout_start', and 'purchase', calculate the conversion rate between each consecutive funnel step for users who entered the funnel in January 2024.

description: Traverse a social graph stored as an edge list to find mutual friends, degrees of separation, or friend-of-friend recommendations. Uses self-joins or recursive CTEs.
schema: friendships(user_id BIGINT, friend_id BIGINT)
example_question: Find all pairs of users who are exactly 2 degrees of separation apart (i.e., share at least one mutual friend but are not direct friends themselves), and return the count of mutual friends for each pair.

description: Identify and remove duplicate ad impression records that arise from delivery system retries, double-counting in aggregation pipelines, or log replay. Uses ROW_NUMBER or DISTINCT on a composite dedup key.
schema: ad_impressions(impression_id VARCHAR, user_id BIGINT, ad_id BIGINT, campaign_id BIGINT, impression_ts TIMESTAMP, revenue DECIMAL)
example_question: The ad_impressions table contains duplicate rows caused by a pipeline replay. Deduplicate impressions using impression_id as the primary key, keeping the earliest record when duplicates exist, and return the total deduplicated revenue per campaign.

description: Group a stream of timestamped user events into discrete sessions where inactivity exceeding a threshold (commonly 30 minutes) marks a session boundary. Uses LAG + running SUM to assign session IDs.
schema: user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_type VARCHAR, event_ts TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Sessionize user_events using a 30-minute inactivity timeout. Return each session's user_id, session number (per user), session start and end timestamps, total duration in minutes, and number of events.

description: Group users by their signup period (cohort) and measure what fraction remain active at fixed intervals after signup (Day 1, Day 7, Day 30). Core metric for evaluating product stickiness.
schema: signups(user_id BIGINT, signup_date DATE), user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_ts TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Build a cohort retention table showing, for each weekly signup cohort in Q1 2024, the retention rates at Day 1, Day 7, and Day 30 after signup.

description: Assign credit for a conversion to the advertising touchpoints that preceded it. First-touch gives full credit to the earliest campaign interaction; last-touch gives full credit to the most recent one before conversion.
schema: ad_clicks(user_id BIGINT, campaign_id BIGINT, click_ts TIMESTAMP), conversions(user_id BIGINT, conversion_ts TIMESTAMP, revenue DECIMAL)
example_question: For each conversion, identify the first-touch and last-touch campaign. Return the total attributed revenue per campaign under both models and the difference between them.

description: Compute a composite engagement score for content items to determine News Feed ranking order. Typically a weighted sum of interaction signals (likes, comments, shares, watch time) normalized by content age.
schema: posts(post_id BIGINT, user_id BIGINT, created_at TIMESTAMP), post_interactions(post_id BIGINT, user_id BIGINT, interaction_type VARCHAR, interaction_ts TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Calculate a ranking score for each post in the last 7 days using the formula: (likes * 1 + comments * 3 + shares * 5) / (hours_since_post + 2)^1.5. Return the top 100 posts ordered by score descending.

description: Compare metric distributions between control and treatment groups in an experiment, compute the difference in means, and assess statistical significance using a z-test or t-test approximation in SQL.
schema: experiment_assignments(user_id BIGINT, experiment_id VARCHAR, variant VARCHAR, assigned_at TIMESTAMP), user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_type VARCHAR, event_ts TIMESTAMP)
example_question: For experiment 'feed_ranking_v2', compute the average number of sessions per user per day for control vs. treatment variants over a 14-day observation window. Report the absolute lift, relative lift, and whether the result is significant at p < 0.05 using a z-test approximation.

description: Track pipeline run history to detect SLA breaches: jobs that ran longer than expected, finished later than their deadline, or failed to run at all within a scheduled window.
schema: pipeline_runs(run_id BIGINT, pipeline_name VARCHAR, started_at TIMESTAMP, finished_at TIMESTAMP, status VARCHAR, expected_duration_minutes INT, sla_deadline TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Identify all pipeline runs in the last 30 days that breached their SLA, either by exceeding expected_duration_minutes or finishing after sla_deadline. Return the pipeline name, breach type, overage amount, and the previous successful run time for context.

description: Detect anomalous data points in a metric time series by comparing each value against a rolling baseline. Values beyond N standard deviations from the rolling mean are flagged as anomalies.
schema: daily_metrics(metric_date DATE, metric_name VARCHAR, metric_value DOUBLE)
example_question: Using a 28-day rolling window, flag daily metric values that deviate by more than 3 standard deviations from the rolling mean. Return the date, metric name, actual value, rolling mean, rolling std, and the z-score.

description: Recommend new connections to a user by finding users who are 2 hops away in the friendship graph (friends of friends who are not already direct friends), ranked by the number of mutual friends shared.
schema: friendships(user_id BIGINT, friend_id BIGINT)
example_question: For user 12345, generate a ranked list of recommended friends (people-you-may-know) by finding all users reachable in exactly 2 hops who are not already friends with user 12345, ordered by descending mutual friend count.

description: Roll up metrics through a geographic or organizational hierarchy (e.g., city → region → country → global) using GROUP BY ROLLUP or GROUPING SETS to produce all levels in a single pass.
schema: user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_ts TIMESTAMP), users(user_id BIGINT, city VARCHAR, region VARCHAR, country VARCHAR)
example_question: Compute daily active users at the city, region, and country level simultaneously for January 2024 using a single query. Include subtotals at each hierarchy level and indicate which rows are subtotals.

description: Track historical changes to user attributes (e.g., country, subscription tier) by maintaining a versioned dimension table with valid_from and valid_to timestamps. Query point-in-time attribute values using range joins.
schema: user_attributes_history(user_id BIGINT, attribute_name VARCHAR, attribute_value VARCHAR, valid_from TIMESTAMP, valid_to TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Determine each user's subscription tier at the time of their first purchase. The user_attributes_history table tracks tier changes with valid_from/valid_to ranges; the purchases table records (user_id, purchase_ts, amount).

description: Design a pipeline strategy that correctly incorporates events that arrive after the processing window has closed. Involves reprocessing logic, watermarking, or upsert patterns to update already-published metrics.
schema: user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_ts TIMESTAMP, processed_ts TIMESTAMP, event_type VARCHAR)
example_question: Your daily DAU pipeline runs at midnight but events for a given day can arrive up to 3 days late. Design a strategy to detect and incorporate late-arriving events into already-published daily DAU metrics without double-counting users.

description: Route records that fail validation or processing to a separate dead letter queue (DLQ) for inspection and potential reprocessing. Prevents bad records from blocking the main pipeline while preserving them for debugging.
schema: pipeline_events(event_id VARCHAR, payload JSON, received_ts TIMESTAMP), dlq_events(event_id VARCHAR, payload JSON, failure_reason VARCHAR, failed_ts TIMESTAMP, retry_count INT)
example_question: Design the DLQ handling logic for an event ingestion pipeline: events that fail schema validation or have null required fields should be written to dlq_events with a failure_reason. Describe the retry strategy and when records should be permanently abandoned.

description: Design ETL jobs so that re-running them produces the same result as running them once, preventing duplicates or incorrect aggregates when jobs are retried due to failures.
schema: raw_events(event_id VARCHAR, user_id BIGINT, event_ts TIMESTAMP), aggregated_daily(date DATE, user_id BIGINT, event_count BIGINT, last_updated TIMESTAMP)
example_question: Rewrite a daily aggregation job that currently appends to a target table so that it becomes idempotent: re-running for any given date should produce exactly the same output as the first run, with no duplicates.

pattern_name: Partitioning Strategy for user_events at Scale
description: Choose an appropriate partitioning scheme for a multi-trillion row user_events table to optimize query performance and storage costs. Considerations include partition pruning, data skew, and compaction strategies.
schema: user_events(user_id BIGINT, event_type VARCHAR, event_ts TIMESTAMP, properties MAP<VARCHAR,VARCHAR>)
example_question: The user_events table has 5TB of new data per day and is queried 95% of the time with a date filter. Propose a partitioning and clustering strategy, explain the trade-offs of partitioning by event_ts date vs. by user_id hash, and describe how you would handle hot partitions.

description: Record and query the provenance of data artifacts: which upstream tables and pipeline jobs produced each downstream table, enabling impact analysis when a source changes and root-cause analysis of data quality issues.
schema: lineage_edges(source_table VARCHAR, target_table VARCHAR, pipeline_job VARCHAR, run_id VARCHAR, run_ts TIMESTAMP), table_catalog(table_name VARCHAR, owner VARCHAR, sla_tier VARCHAR)
example_question: A source table 'raw_user_events' has been identified as having corrupted data for January 15. Write a query to find all downstream tables that were derived from raw_user_events for that date's pipeline run, ordered by their distance from the source in the lineage graph.

description: Manage changes to table schemas over time (adding columns, changing types, removing columns) without breaking downstream consumers. Involves backward/forward compatibility strategies, schema registry usage, and migration patterns.
schema: events_v1(event_id VARCHAR, user_id BIGINT, event_ts TIMESTAMP), events_v2(event_id VARCHAR, user_id BIGINT, event_ts TIMESTAMP, country_code VARCHAR, app_version VARCHAR)
example_question: Your team wants to add two new columns (country_code, app_version) to a 50TB Iceberg table that is read by 20 downstream pipelines. Describe the end-to-end migration plan including: how to backfill historical data, how to ensure downstream pipelines continue working during the transition, and how to validate completeness after migration.
